coredata.dc:title: Cross-linked gelatin film enriched with green carbon quantum dots for bioactive food packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Khoshkalampour, Ali
Ghorbani, Marjan
Ghasempour, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: Dialdehyde Persian gum (PG) was applied to reinforce the physicomechanical properties of gelatin films through cross-linkage. Carbon dots (CDs) from grape leaves were incorporated into the system to fabricate nanocomposites. Adding oxidized PG to gelatin and CDs up to 5 % improved tensile strength. The solubility of the oxidized PG/gelatin film increased with the addition of CDs. The water vapor transmission of the gelatin film decreased through cross-linking. Adding CDs to the films reduced UV transfer rates but increased the antioxidant activity from 16.66 to 94.08 %. The oxidized PG/gelatin film with CDs showed antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. The fabricated films showed no cell cytotoxicity. Observed decreases in TVB-N and TBARS values, in trout fillets coated with the film containing 30 % CDs, resulted in extended shelf life up to 4 days. Cross-linked gelatin incorporated with CDs offers various applications in the food industry due to its functional properties.

head.ce:title: Cross-linked gelatin film enriched with green carbon quantum dots for bioactive food packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dialdehyde Persian gum (PG) was applied to reinforce the physicomechanical properties of gelatin films through cross-linkage. Carbon dots (CDs) from grape leaves were incorporated into the system to fabricate nanocomposites. Adding oxidized PG to gelatin and CDs up to 5 % improved tensile strength. The solubility of the oxidized PG/gelatin film increased with the addition of CDs. The water vapor transmission of the gelatin film decreased through cross-linking. Adding CDs to the films reduced UV transfer rates but increased the antioxidant activity from 16.66 to 94.08 %. The oxidized PG/gelatin film with CDs showed antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. The fabricated films showed no cell cytotoxicity. Observed decreases in TVB-N and TBARS values, in trout fillets coated with the film containing 30 % CDs, resulted in extended shelf life up to 4 days. Cross-linked gelatin incorporated with CDs offers various applications in the food industry due to its functional properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging prevents spoilage, increases storage time, and facilitates food transportation. Despite awareness and concerns about the long recycling time and damage of plastic waste to the environment, human and animal health, and the production of micro and nano plastics, plastic is still the most common material for food packaging. It inflicts 80–120 million dollars in losses on the global economy annually; these reasons are why researchers have taken immense interest in biodegradable materials (Barron & Sparks, 2020; Wu, Misra, & Mohanty, 2021). Biodegradable packaging materials are mostly made from proteins and polysaccharides (Tang, Zheng, Yang, & Li, 2022). Gelatin is an animal protein made from the fibrous insoluble collagen found in bones and skin, formed as waste after the slaughter and processing of animals, with various applications in the food, pharmaceutical, and photography industries (Guo, Ge, Li, Mu, & Li, 2014).

Gelatin has a good ability to form films owing to its aromatic amino acids and high flexibility; it is also biodegradable, biocompatible, and non-toxic. However, its limitations include low heat resistance, water vapor penetration, solubility in water, and poor mechanical properties compared with conventional packaging (Kumar, Shukla, Baul, Mitra, & Halder, 2018). To improve these properties, gelatin can be modified by cross-linkage using several chemical agents, including genipin, diisocyanates, glutaraldehyde, carbodiimide, or natural polymer-based cross-linkers (Mu, Guo, Li, Lin, & Li, 2012). Crosslinking gelatin with oxidized polysaccharides is a safe way to reduce the limitations of gelatin for packaging. With sodium periodate, the adjacent diols of polysaccharides can be easily oxidized to two aldehyde groups. Active aldehyde groups react with the ε-amino groups in gelatin (Schiff base reaction), facilitating the formation of a stable structure and improving gelatin's properties (Mousazadeh, Ehsani, Kia, & Ghasempour, 2021). Researchers have attempted to improve the properties of gelatin film by using sodium periodate in the production of dialdehyde gums and disaccharides such as carboxymethyl cellulose (Mu, Guo, Li, Lin, & Li, 2012), xanthan gum (Guo, Ge, Li, Mu, & Li, 2014), guar gum (Maroufi, Ghorbani, & Tabibiazar, 2020), and sucrose (Mousazadeh, Ehsani, Kia, & Ghasempour, 2021).

PG, also known as Zedo or Angum, is a cheap and available natural polysaccharide derived from wild almond trees such as Amygdalus scoparia Spach. It offers a wide range of applications in the pharmaceutical and food industries as emulsifiers, stabilizers, encapsulating agents, etc. PG contains two soluble and insoluble fractions, which are composed mainly of arabinose and galactose monosaccharides with trace amounts of xylose, rhamnose and mannose (Dabestani, Kadkhodaee, Phillips, & Abbasi, 2018). It is feasible to develop new polysaccharide derivatives by modifying PG via cross-linking interactions due to functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine in their structure. Dialdehyde formation in PG was reported through using sodium methaperiodate which shows PG potential for interaction with proteins (Mozafari, Hojjatoleslamy, & Mohammadizadeh, 2021).

Traditional packaging cannot meet the current demands of food packaging due to lacking antimicrobial, antioxidant, and UV inhibitory properties. Researchers have recently considered using different types of nanoparticles in active packaging due to their high reactivity and efficiency (Sun, Jiang, Wu, Tong, Pang, & Wu, 2020). CDs are fluorescent nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size, first characterized by Xu, Ray, Gu, Ploehn, Gearheart, Raker, et al. (2004). CDs are easily synthesized and have electrical and chemical stability, low toxicity, ecological safety, good water solubility, good photoluminescence, and optical characteristics with suitable functional properties (Lin, Huang, Yang, Zeng, Luo, Huang, et al., 2022; Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2021). Recently, the synthesis of CDs from natural sources has attracted the attention of food scientists for identifying frauds, heavy metals, bacteria, toxins, and pesticides (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018; Vijeata, Chaudhary, & Chaudhary, 2022), increasing the oxidative stability in frying oils (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2021), prolonging shelf-life (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020), and developing food packaging materials (Moradi, Molaei, Kousheh, Guimarães, & McClements, 2021). However, there are few studies on using CDs in active food packaging. This study aimed to develop cross-linked PG/gelatin film reinforced with CDs. Grape leaves were used to produce the CD nanoparticles. We hypothesized that these nanoparticles would help preserve food products by improving the antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV-blocking properties of PG/gelatin food packaging films. The ability of the fabricated nanocomposite to extend food shelf life was investigated in trout fillets via the thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) and total volatile bases nitrogen (TVB-N) tests.

Grape leaves and PG were purchased from a local store (Tabriz, Iran). Sodium meta periodate (NaIO4), bovine gelatin, calcium chloride (CaCl2), boric acid (H3BO3), magnesium oxide (MgO), malondialdehyde, thiobarbituric acid, and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were acquired from Merck Co. (Germany). Violet red bile agar, Baird-Parker agar, glycerol, ethylene glycol, hydroxylamine hydrochloride, trichloroacetic acid, and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). Bacterial species Staphylococcus aureus (PTCC-1015) and Escherichia coli (PTCC-1270) were acquired from the Pasteur Institute of Iran (Tehran, Iran). Finally, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was procured from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA).

The CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal process based on the method of Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, Salavati-Niasari, and Ghanbari (2018) with some modifications. Grape leaves were ground after being dried in an oven at 40 °C for 12 h. Then, 3 g of dried grape leaf powder was poured into 150 mL of distilled water and dispersed for 10 min using an ultrasound bath. The prepared solution was put into a 200 mL hydrothermal autoclave reactor (the outer part of which was made of steel and the inner part of which was made of Teflon™) and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h, then cooled to ambient temperature. The brown solution was centrifuged at 4500 g for 15 min to remove black particles, after which the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain the powder of CDs.

The size of CDs was measured using a particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Worcestershire, UK). A transmission electron microscope (LEO 906, Zeiss, Germany) examined the morphology. The functional groups of CDs were identified by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Brucker, Germany). A spectrophotometer (PHARMACIA BIOTECH, Ultrospect 2000, England) was used to measure the CDs' ultraviolet (UV)/visible absorbance. A spectrofluorometer detected the excitation and emission of fluorescence (FP-750, Jasco, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of water-soluble Persian gum and oxidized Persian gum (PG)
ce:section.ce:para: To separate the water-soluble component, first, we separated and ground the colorless gums and then sifted (mesh No. 100) the obtained powder. PG (2 % w/w) was mixed on a magnetic stirrer for 4 h at 80 °C in distilled water and stored overnight at 4 °C. The prepared solution was centrifuged at 4500 g for 30 min; the supernatant was dried in an oven at 40 °C for 24 h to obtain a water-soluble powder.

2.5 g of PG was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water (5 % w/w) on a magnetic stirrer to oxidize PG. Then, 3 g of sodium periodate was dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water, added to the gum solution in the dark, and mixed at ambient temperature for 6 h to perform the reaction. Finally, 1 mL of ethylene glycol was added to the solution to inactive sodium periodate. The final solution was dialyzed by a dialysis bag (MWCO = 12,000 Da) for 24 h (Maroufi, Ghorbani, & Tabibiazar, 2020).

The aldehyde content of the oxidized PG was measured by turning aldehyde groups into oximes using hydroxylamine hydrochloride in Schiff's base reaction, according to Ali, Ganie, and Mazumdar (2018). Oxidized PG (0.2 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water, and a similar amount of PG was added to 20 mL of distilled water for the blank sample. Then, the pH of each solution was adjusted to 5 with 0.5 M sodium hydroxide solution. After that, 20 mL of a hydroxylamine hydrochloride solution (0.72 mol/L) was added to the solutions and incubated in a shaker incubator at 40 °C for 24 h. Finally, titration was performed using a 0.5 M sodium hydroxide solution with phenolphthalein as the indicator, and the volume of the solution consumed was recorded in liters (Vo for oxidized PG and Vb for PG or the blank). The aldehyde content of oxidized PG was determined using the following equation:
Aldehydecontent % =
V o - V b
× M N a O H
m
Mw
× 100
where MNaOH = 0.5 M, m is the dry weight cof oxidized PG (0.2 g), and the molecular weight (Mw) is 211 g/mol, according to a previous report (Mozafari, Hojjatoleslamy, & Mohammadizadeh, 2021).

Initially, the oxidized PG was diluted to 50 % v/v with distilled water, and a 5 % solution of bovine gelatin (Type B, Bloom 240) was prepared on a magnetic stirrer at 40 °C. Glycerol (40 % dry weight of gelatin) was used as a plasticizer. The film solution was prepared with different dry weight ratios of oxidized PG: gelatin 1:4, 1:2, and 1:1, which were coded as (A1), (A2), and (A3), respectively. The resulting solution was poured into a plate (8 cm) and left to dry at room temperature for 48 h. Different concentrations of CDs (2.5 (AC-2.5), 5 (AC-5), 7.5 (AC-7.5), 15 (AC-15), and 30 % w/w (AC-30)) were added to the sample, which was significantly different from the control sample in terms of mechanical properties. A control film was prepared with PG (without oxidation) and gelatin.

Mechanical properties, including tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB), were measured using a texture analyzer (Gotech Testing Machines, AI 7000, Taiwan) fitted with a 1 kN load cell according to ASTM D638-91 standards. At a speed of 5 mm/min, the films were cut in the form of dumbbells with a size of 70 × 10 mm with a gripping distance of 50 mm, and the samples were conditioned for 24 h at 23 °C and 50 % relative humidity (the mechanical test was performed in triplicate) (El-Sayed, El-Sayed, Ibrahim, & Youssef, 2020). The functional groups of A3 film were identified by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The samples were analyzed with a TGA device to determine the decomposition temperature and thermal stability of the produced films (Linseis, STA PT1600, Germany) under a nitrogen environment. Heating was done from 25 to 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min.

The films were cut into a circle with a diameter of 2 cm and dried at 105 °C for 8 h. The oven-dried films were weighed, then immersed in 50 mL of distilled water and shaken for 24 h at 150 rpm at room temperature. Then, the samples were dried at 105 °C to reach fixed weights. Finally, the WS of films was calculated by the following equation:
WS
% =
Initial
d r y
m a s s - F i n a l
d r y
m a s s
Initial
d r y
m a s s
× 100

The films already cut into a diameter of 1.5 cm were placed on the top of individual vials (5 mL; 1 cm diameter) containing 1.5 g of CaCl2 and sealed with parafilm. The vials were placed in a desiccator containing 50 mL of water at room temperature (25 °C) so that one side was at 75 % relative humidity (RH2) and the other side at 0 % (RH1). The weight of the vials was recorded every 2 h for 12 h, and their slope was calculated (s) in the end. The WVP was calculated using the following equation:
WVP =
S × d
A × P ( R H 2 - R H 1 )
where d is the thickness (mm), A is the area (mm2), and P is the saturation vapor pressure of water at room temperature (0.0313 atm) (Wang, Liu, Qi, Sun, Jiang, Li, et al., 2022).

The DPPH scavenging activity test was performed according to the procedure described by Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, and Han (2022), with some modifications. To assess the antioxidant activity of the CDs, different concentrations of CDs (2.5, 5, 7.5, 15, and 30 % w/v) were prepared in distilled water, and the CD solutions were diluted five times. To assess the films’ antioxidant activity, 50 mg of each film was soaked in 3 mL of distilled water for 24 h. Then, 400 μL of each sample was mixed well with 3 mL of 0.06 mM DPPH using a vortex, before being placed in the dark for 1 h. To prepare the control sample, 400 μL of film-free distilled water was combined with 3 mL of DPPH. Finally, the absorbance of the samples was measured at 517 nm with a spectrophotometer.

To investigate the accelerated release of nanoparticles in the films, we measured the inactivation of DPPH every hour for 7 h. The temperature was set at 30 °C during the test (Łupina, Kowalczyk, Lis, Raszkowska-Kaczor, & Drozłowska, 2022). The inhibition of DPPH was calculated based on the following equation:
D P P H
i n h i b i t i o n =
Abs
o f
t h e
c o n t r o l - A b s
o f
t h e
s a m p l e
Abs
o f
t h e
c o n t r o l
× 100

To examine the antibacterial properties of the CDs, the well diffusion method was used in a solid medium. Using a sterile swab, an inoculum of 0.5 Mcfarland turbidity (1.5 × 108 CFU/ml) of bacterial suspension was applied to the surface of the agar plates. Wells with a diameter of 8 mm were punched on Mueller-Hinton agar, filled with 30 μL of different concentrations of CDs (0.5, 1, 1.5, 3.5, 7.5, and 15 %), and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Finally, the diameter of the inhibitory zone was measured (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022). The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) against the E. coli and S. aureus pathogens were determined as described by Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, Roy, Min, et al. (2022), with some modifications. The first step was to prepare diluted CD samples (100 μL) in 96-well plates using the serial dilution method, with a concentration range of 0.05 to 15 %. The serially diluted CDs array was filled with 100 μL sterile Mueller-Hinton broth medium. After that, 10 mL of bacterial broth with 0.5 Mcfarland turbidity (1.5 × 108 CFU/ml) was added to each well, and the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 20 h. Then, each well was filled with 20 μL of resazurin solution (0.4 mg/mL), a redox indicator, then incubated for 4 h. An ELISA microplate reader was used to evaluate the amount of bacterial growth in each well by measuring the change in optical density at 595 nm. Wells with a significant decline in optical density values were used to calculate the MIC. By subculturing the bacterial solutions from the wells close to the MIC zone on brain heart infusion agar plates, the MBC was determined.

To investigate the antimicrobial property of AC-30 film, both sides of the film were sterilized under UV light. Then, 50 mg of each film was added to 2 mL of bacterial suspension with 0.5 McFarland turbidity, shaken well, and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, 100 μL of S. aureus (PTCC-1015) and E. coli (PTCC-1270) was added to Baird-Parker agar (containing 5 % egg yolk and 1 % potassium tellurite) and violet red bile agar in tenfold gradient dilutions, respectively. Then, the microbial suspensions were dispersed on the culture mediums using a capillary tube. After 24 h of incubation, bacterial viability was calculated by counting colonies (Xiao, Liu, Kang, Wang, & Xu, 2020).

The transmittance of UV and visible light through the films was measured with a spectrophotometer at 200–700 nm wavelengths. The baseline was recorded with an empty quartz cuvette, and then UV/visible transmission was recorded for the films in the cuvette. The average transmittance was calculated in the ranges of 230–280 (TUV-C), 315–400 (TUV-A), 280–315 (TUV-B), and 400–760 (TVisible) nm (Patil, Waghmare, Pawar, Salunkhe, Kolekar, Sohn, et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022).

The biocompatibility of the A3, AC-2.5, AC-5, AC-7.5, AC-15, and AC-30 films was assessed against a normal cell line (endothelial cell line, HUVEC) using the MTT cell proliferation reagent. The films were cut into circular disks 15 mm in diameter, sterilized, and placed on a 24-well plate. After that, 1 mL of cell suspension containing 1 × 105 cells was seeded on each sample and then incubated for 24, 48, and 72 h at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. Then, 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide solvent was poured into the sample-containing wells before another 4 h of incubation. As a result, the products of insoluble purple formazan were dissolved, and the supernatant was examined optically by the ELISA microplate reader at the wavenumber of 570 nm. The control group was considered as NIH3T3 fibroblast cells without any treatment (Elx808, Biotek, USA) (Aman Mohammadi, Ramezani, Hosseini, Mortazavian, Hosseini, & Ghorbani, 2021).

Fresh trout fillets, divided into equal parts and weighing 30 g, were covered with layers of A3 and AC-30 in dimensions of 7x7 cm. The control sample was not packaged. To investigate the shelf life, thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) and total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) were examined during 12 days of storage at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total volatile basic nitrogen and thiobarbituric acid reactive substances
ce:section.ce:para: The TVB-N and TBARS assays were performed according to Kulawik, Jamróz, Zając, Guzik, and Tkaczewska (2019), with some modifications. To assess TVB-N, 2.5 g of each sample was homogenized with 100 mL of 3 % MgO solution (v/w), and it was distilled for 10 min in 25 mL of 4 % boric acid solution using an automatic Kjeldahl distillation unit (Koreatech, KR-DIS-Auto, Korea). Then, using 0.1 M sulfuric acid, the pH was brought to 4.6, the acid consumed was recorded, and the amount of TVB-N was calculated using the following equation:

T V B - N
( m g / 100 g
f r e s h
f i s h )
=
V × C × 14 × 100
w
where V is the volume of sulfuric acid consumed, C is the concentration of sulfuric acid, and W is the sample weight.

To investigate TBARS, 2.5 g of each sample was homogenized with 10 mL of chloroform; after that, 5 mL of TBRAS solution (7.5 g of trichloroacetic acid and 0.375 g of thiobarbituric acid, reaching a volume of 100 mL with 0.25 M HCl; then, 2 mL of 2 % ethanolic BHT solution was added). The solutions were placed in a 90 °C water bath for 15 min, then cooled with tap water. The solutions were filtered using Whatman no. 1 filter paper. Then, the absorbance was measured at 532 nm. The concentration of TBARS was calculated using the standard curve of malondialdehyde (MDA) at different concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 mg/kg, R2 = 0.998).

The effects of cross-linking and different amounts of CDs (independent variables in Table 1 ) on packaging film properties were investigated using analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Tukey's multiple tests with a 95 % confidence interval with GraphPad Prism 9 software. The experiments were repeated three times.

We characterized the synthesized CDs via fluorescence, dynamic light scattering (DLS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and UV–visible light absorption analysis. The best spectrofluorometry emission was at 520 nm, while the excitation was at 430 nm (Fig. 1 a). Lin et al. (2022) reported that the intensity and maximum emission depended on the excitation. As shown in Fig. 1b, As the excitation increased from 390 to 500 nm, the emission showed a slight shift from 504 to 560 nm. The short shift of the fluorescence peak suggested that the particle size distribution was narrow and led to lower energy paths for emission (Fu, Liu, Liu, Chen, Lin, Zheng, et al., 2022). The synthesized CDs were brown under visible light but green when exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 1a), consistent with the emission spectrum in Fig. 1a (green line).

The DLS analysis showed that the CDs averaged 4.6 ± 0.63 nm in size, corresponding to the specified size of CDs (below 10 nm); also, TEM images showed that the CDs were almost spherical and dispersed, and their size was consistent with the DLS results (Fig. 1c, d).

The UV–visible light absorption spectrum showed a peak at 275 nm, ascribed to the n–π* transition of C═O and the π–π* transition of C═C (Xu, Yang, Li, Zhao, & Liao, 2015). This peak shows that the CDs had aromatic sp2 carbon domains in their structure, allowing them to absorb UV radiation (Fu et al., 2022; Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2021).

Sodium periodate oxidizes polysaccharides by breaking C2─C3 bonds, and aldehyde groups can cross-link with Ɛ-amino groups of lysine or hydroxylysine side groups of gelatin to form a Schiff base, ultimately improving the properties of gelatin (Guo, Ge, Li, Mu, & Li, 2014). The aldehyde content of the PG solution after oxidation was 64 %. Previous studies reported different aldehyde contents in different gums after oxidation: xanthan gum (7.8–45 %) (Guo, Ge, Li, Mu, & Li, 2014), gum arabic (15.19–19.68 %) (Ali, Ganie, & Mazumdar, 2018), kappa carrageenan (69 %) (Akrami-Hasan-Kohal, Ghorbani, Mahmoodzadeh, & Nikzad, 2020), and carboxymethylcellulose (30–80 %) (Li, Wu, Mu, & Lin, 2011). This difference in results may be due to differences in treatment conditions (e.g., pH and temperature), polysaccharide structures, the mole ratio of sodium periodate, and reaction time (Li, Wu, Mu, & Lin, 2011).

Improving the mechanical properties of the gelatin film is essential to ensure that it can endure external stress and maintain its integrity throughout its use in food packaging. The TS values of gelatin, PG/gelatin, and A3 films were 4.62, 5.86, and 20.24 MPa, respectively. The addition of oxidized PG to gelatin resulted in a rigid structure with a high TS of 20.24 ± 6.12 MPa and low EB of 6.085 ± 0.2 % (Table 1). This phenomenon can be attributed to cross-linking between dialdehyde groups of oxidized PG and amino groups of gelatin, which is frequently accompanied by decreased EB (Mu, Guo, Li, Lin, & Li, 2012). The thickness of the films was presented in Table S1 (in supplementary data).

The addition of CDs to the 5 % dry weight of the films increased their TS compared with the A3 film due to the good dispersion and hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of CDs and the carboxylic and amino groups of PG and gelatin (Ahmadi, Hasanzadeh, & Ghasempour, 2023; Fu et al., 2022; Staroszczyk, Sztuka, Wolska, Wojtasz-Pająk, & Kołodziejska, 2014). The addition of more than 5 % of CDs caused a significant reduction in TS (p < 0.05), possibly due to the aggregation of CDs (Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Javanbakht and Namazi (2017) reported that TS increased when adding up to 17 % graphene CDs to starch films but decreased with higher percentages. Likewise, Wang et al. (2022) reported an increase in the TS of chitosan films by adding up to 3 % CDs, beyond which a decrease was recorded. Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, and Molaei (2020) reported that all concentrations of CDs decreased the TS of nanocellulose and increased its EB. Similarly, Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, and Han (2022) did not report significant changes in the TS of cellulose nanofibers after adding CDs. These different results may be due to the different functional groups present on the surface of CDs, the concentration and type of CDs, and the properties of the matrix of the films (Moradi et al., 2021).

The functional groups of CDs, gelatin, PG, oxidized PG, A3 film, and the A3 film containing 30 % CDs (AC-30) were identified via FTIR analysis (Fig. 2 A). The spectrum of CDs showed a peak at 3423 cm−1, indicating the OH stretching vibration and NH stretching of amine. The band around 2926 cm−1 is attributed to CH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1705 and 1634 cm−1 indicate the C═O and C═C stretching bending vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1403 cm−1 is related to the OH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1056 and 1110 cm−1 correspond to the CO and COH stretching vibrations, respectively (Wang et al., 2022; Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020). These results indicate that the synthesized CDs contained hydroxyl, amine, and carbonyl groups, which cause the good solubility of CDs in water without the need for chemical alterations.

The spectrum of gelatin showed peaks at 3360 cm−1 assigned to amide A; at 3072 cm−1, 2952 cm−1, and 2866 cm−1 attributed to amide B; 1641 cm−1 related to amide I; 1544 and 1437 cm−1 ascribed to amide II; and 1240 cm−1 assigned to amide III (Staroszczyk, Sztuka, Wolska, Wojtasz-Pająk, & Kołodziejska, 2014; Tang, Zheng, Yang, & Li, 2022). The spectrum of PG showed peaks at 3400 cm−1 related to the OH groups, while those at 2918 cm−1 represent the asymmetric stretching of CH2 groups. The band around 1617 cm−1 is related to the carboxyl groups. Moreover, the peaks at 1031 cm−1 and 876 cm−1 indicate CC and CH groups, respectively (Dabestani, Kadkhodaee, Phillips, & Abbasi, 2018).

Following the production of aldehyde groups in PG, the peaks that appeared at 1736 and 880 cm−1 correspond to the carbonyl of aldehyde groups (C═O) and the formation of hemiacetal bonds between the aldehyde groups and nearby hydroxyl groups (OH), respectively (Li, Wu, Mu, & Lin, 2011).

The FTIR spectrum of the A3 film revealed the band's disappearance at 1736 cm−1, with a new peak appearing at 1715 cm−1, related to the formation of new amide groups. This new peak demonstrates that the aldehyde groups of PG and the amide groups of gelatin are covalently cross-linked, as also stated by Akrami-Hasan-Kohal, Ghorbani, Mahmoodzadeh, and Nikzad (2020). The location of the peak changed by adding CDs to the A3 film, after which the OH wavelength shifted from 3432 cm−1 to 3420 cm−1, indicating the formation of hydrogen bonds between the film and CDs (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022; Wang et al., 2022). Moreover, adding CDs to the A3 film resulted in larger peaks associated with the hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl and carboxyl groups), representing an increase in the hydrophilic functional groups of the film after the addition of CDs. The formation of hydrogen bonds between CD nanoparticles and coated materials like chitosan (Wang et al., 2022), nanocellulose (Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020), starch (Koshy, Koshy, Mary, Sadanandan, Jisha, & Pothan, 2021), and pectin/gelatin (Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2021), was also reported previously.

The PG/gelatin, A3, and AC-30 films all showed three-stage mass loss as a function of temperature (Fig. 2B). The first mass loss step occurred at 25–150 °C due to the evaporation of the bound and free water in the films. The decomposition of glycerol and low-molecular-weight proteins in gelatin occurred in the second part of the thermal degradation at 155–250 °C. The third thermal degradation is associated with the breakdown of polysaccharides and gelatin with high-molecular-weight proteins, which starts at 255 and continues until 400 °C (Mousazadeh, Ehsani, Kia, & Ghasempour, 2021).

The thermal stability behavior of A3 and AC-30 films differed significantly from the PG/gelatin film. The AC-30 film lost more moisture than the other two films. As Ezati, Roy, and Rhim (2021) reported, the reason for this is that CDs can entrap water molecules. Similarly, Jia, Tang, and Wu (2018) used this feature of CDs to increase the water retention capacity of composite membranes. The thermal degradation pattern in the A3 film shows an improvement in the thermal stability of this film compared with the PG/gelatin film, which is due to the formation of covalent bonds between the dialdehyde groups of PG and gelatin proteins (Akrami-Hasan-Kohal, Ghorbani, Mahmoodzadeh, & Nikzad, 2020). The results also showed that the thermal stability of the AC-30 film increased with the addition of CDs compared with the A3 film. This could be due to hydrogen bonds forming between CDs’ functional groups and the film matrix (Koshy, Koshy, Mary, Sadanandan, Jisha, & Pothan, 2021). According to Fig. 2B, the residual mass values at 600 °C for PG/gelatin, A3, and AC-30 films were 18.3, 21, and 22.6 %, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to examine the microstructure of the films and determine their homogeneity and smoothness. As shown in Fig. 2C, the A3 film featured a smooth, homogenous, and non-porous surface due to cross-linking between PG and gelatin, as opposed to the non-smooth, inhomogeneous, and wrinkled surface of the PG/gelatin film. The addition of 30 % CDs to the polymer matrix (AC-30 film) resulted in aggregation that reduced the homogeneity and smoothness of the surface and cross-sections. Correspondingly, Javanbakht and Namazi (2017); Koshy, Koshy, Mary, Sadanandan, Jisha, and Pothan (2021) reported the aggregation of graphene quantum dots and CDs after addition to packaging films.

To preserve intermediate or high-moisture foods, films with antibacterial/antioxidant properties must be resistant to water. The solubility of all films was significantly (p < 0.05) lower than gelatin and PG/gelatin due to PG and gelatin cross-links (Fig. 3 a). After 24 h, 100 % and 92 % of the gelatin and PG/gelatin films dissolved, respectively, while the solubility of the A3 film was 24 %, increasing with the addition of CDs. The solubilities of the AC-2.5, AC-5, AC-7.5, AC-15, and AC-30 films were 25.1, 27.21, 29.75, 31.65, and 36.1 %, respectively. After adding oxidized PG to gelatin, aldehyde groups can react with the amino groups of gelatin to form cross-linking bonds, which provide a denser structure with less water absorption (Mousazadeh, Ehsani, Kia, & Ghasempour, 2021). Moreover, we showed that the solubility of the films increased with a rise in the content of CDs. The number of hydrophilic functional groups increased with the addition of CDs, augmenting the solubility of these films compared to CD-free films. Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, and Han (2022) also reported an increase in cellulose nanofiber films' solubility after adding CDs due to an increase in hydrophilic functional groups.

The water vapor permeability (WVP) should be as low as feasible because one of the primary functions of food packaging is preventing or reducing moisture transfer between the food and the environment. We found that the WVP of all films decreased significantly compared with the gelatin film (Fig. 3b). The WVP of gelatin decreased from 6.355 × 10−10 gmm/h mm2 Pa to 2.369 × 10−10 gmm/h mm2 Pa and 2.97 × 10−10 gmm/h mm2 Pa in the A3 and PG/gelatin films, respectively. After adding CDs to the A3 film, no significant change was observed. However, an insignificant (p > 0.05) decrease was observed in WVP values after adding 2.5 and 5 % CDs, while the WVP values increased compared with PG/gelatin and A3 films (p > 0.05) by the addition of 15 % and 30 % CDs. A tortuous path formed via cross-linking between dialdehyde PG, gelatin, and CDs, can explain the initial drop in WVP and water vapor diffusion, and the subsequent increase can be due to the presence of plentiful hydroxyl groups in CDs, responsible for their hydrophilic nature. Besides, due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CDs and film ingredients, increased solubility and hydrophilicity were observed in films containing more CDs (Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2021; Lin et al., 2022). Similarly, Wang, et al. (2022) reported that reducing the WVP of chitosan films after adding CDs reduced the free volume between molecular chains and increased air-tightness. Ezati, Roy, and Rhim (2021) reported an increase in WVP after adding CDs to pectin/gelatin-based films, attributed to an increase in the films’ hydrophilic nature.

The controlled release of antioxidants from packaging films helps avoid losing efficacy by prolonging the antioxidant activity. As evident in Fig. S1, the DPPH scavenging capacity in CDs at concentrations of 2.5, 5, 7.5, 15, and 30 % was 24.1, 37.6, 58.9, 85.0, and 92.4 %, respectively, with the latter two concentrations showing significantly higher DPPH scavenging capacity values (p < 0.05).

We found that all A3 films containing CDs had significantly more (p < 0.05) antioxidant activity than the CD-free film. By adding 2.5 to 30 % CDs, a 16.66 to 94.08 % increase in antioxidant activity was observed (Fig. 3c). CDs can inactivate DPPH free radicals by transporting hydrogen, NH2 electrons, and combinations of both (Ji, Sheardy, Zeng, Zhang, Chevva, Allado, et al., 2019). Other studies also reported that the addition of CDs to the packaging films resulted in a strong antioxidant property and the production of active films (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022; Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2021; Koshy, Koshy, Mary, Sadanandan, Jisha, & Pothan, 2021; Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020).

As Łupina, Kowalczyk, Lis, Raszkowska-Kaczor, and Drozłowska (2022) have stated that polymer-carriers release is a multi-day process. The accelerated release test was applied by setting the temperature to 30 °C for the entire test. As shown in Fig. 3d, DPPH scavenging activity increased with time, and the most important factors influencing the release rate are the type of film, structure, and temperature (Ramos, Beltrán, Peltzer, Valente, & Garrigós, 2014). The results show the controlled release of CDs from the films, and these films can be used for oxidation-sensitive foods, extending the shelf-life by retarding oxidation.

The well diffusion test and MIC/MBC measurement methods were used to investigate the antimicrobial properties of CDs against S. aureus and E. coli. As evident in Fig. S2, the inhibitory zone of CDs in concentrations of 15, 7.5, and 3.5 % against E. coli was 11, 7, and 1 mm, respectively. For S. aureus, these values were 12, 10, and 3 mm, respectively. The MIC was 3.5 % and 1.5 % against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. As seen in Fig. S1, the MBC for both bacteria was 3.5 %.

The total viable colony count method was used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of A3 and AC-30 films against E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 4 ). After 24 h, the growth of E. coli and S. aureus incubated with AC-30 was 1.24 and 1.61 log CFU/ml less than the A3 film, respectively. The antibacterial activity of CDs is influenced by functional groups, shape, particle size, and the kind of bacterium. CDs can damage membranes, leading to bacterial mortality because of their small size and high surface-to-volume ratio. CDs cover the cell surface and disrupt it or release free radicals that cause damage to lipids, nucleotides, and proteins (Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020). Also, Li, Huang, Song, Zhang, Wang, Lu, et al. (2018) investigated the effect of CDs on S. aureus and E. coli, observing that the surface of the bacteria is roughened due to being covered with CDs, which prevents the entry of nutrients and their reproduction. In addition, they reported that CDs containing oxygen groups (CO and CO) are absorbed in the cell wall of bacteria before diffusing within them. Then, CDs bind to bacterial DNA and RNA (via non-covalent bonds), alter the secondary structure of DNA and RNA, and affect the genetic processes of bacteria. Furthermore, adsorbed CDs also can damage the bacterial cell wall and cause leakage of cellular contents.

According to the results, the Gram-positive bacterium S. aureus grew less slowly than the Gram-negative bacterium E. coli. These results agree with Travlou, Giannakoudakis, Algarra, Labella, Rodríguez-Castellón, and Bandosz (2018) and could be due to differences in cell walls, which are thicker and more permeable in Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative bacteria.

Transparent packaging helps the consumer to assess the product's visual quality quickly. On the other hand, UV initiates oxidation in food, which causes nutritional oxidation, unpleasant taste, discoloration, and the formation of hazardous chemicals. The transparencies of gelatin, PG/gelatin, and A3 films were 81.75, 90.18, and 80.1 %, respectively. Adding CDs to the films reduced their transparency, and the transparencies of AC-2.5, AC-5, AC-7.5, AC-15, and AC-30 films were 75.4, 53.19, 39.5, 35, and 19 %, respectively (Fig. S3a). As shown in Fig. S3b, the films were placed on a sheet and the printed text was visible, indicating the proper transparency of the films. However, increasing the number of CDs significantly reduced the UV-A and UV-B transfer rates compared with gelatin, PG/gelatin, and A3 films (Table S2. in supplementary data). Although the UV-blocking mechanism of CDs remains elusive, this ability may be attributed to various functional groups on the surface of CDs. Moreover, the electron transition of n to π* or π to π* orbitals is provided by altered functional groups. Sufficient conjugated structures can generate photostability and UV-light-blocking activity in the film (Moradi et al., 2021). Besides, the UV-blocking properties of CDs have been demonstrated in diverse packaging materials, including polyvinyl alcohol (Patil et al., 2020; Xu, Gao, Xu, Fang, Xu, & Zhang, 2021), pectin/gelatin (Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2021), cellulose nanofibers (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022), and chitosan (Wang et al., 2022). As the CDs synthesized from the grape leaves had a fluorescence property, the photographs of the films in the UV chamber (Ultraviolet Products, CA, USA) with a wavelength of 365 nm showed that the films became brighter with increasing CDs (Fig. S3c). Accordingly, Due to their fluorescence and UV barrier properties, these films can be used for smart packaging and oxidation-sensitive foods.

Cytotoxicity is a critical factor in determining the film's safety for use in food packaging. The viability of HUVEC cells after 24, 48, and 72 h are shown in Fig. S4 (in supplementary data). As shown in the results, no significant difference was noted between the control and films, and the viability values were higher than 86.8 %. The obtained results exhibited that, in all films, the addition of CDs increased cell viability. The reason for this proliferation in films containing CDs can depend on the increased hydrophilicity of the films; Tihan, Ionita, Popescu, and Iordachescu (2009) also showed that cells could grow better on hydrophilic surfaces than on hydrophobic surfaces. The present showed that films with higher CD levels had increased solubility and hydrophilicity, which is directly related to the increase in proliferation.

The cell viability for all samples did not significantly (p > 0.05) change when increasing the incubation time to 72 h. Also, an insignificant decrease in the viability of cells treated with AC-30 film was observed after 72 h compared to other films (viability 90 %). The decrease in cell viability can be due to the disruption of cell proliferation and damage to cell organelles, such as the cell wall, due to the high CD content in the environment (Esfandiari, Bagheri, Ehtesabi, Fatahi, Tavana, & Latifi, 2019). However, the cell viability for films containing CDs was higher than 90 % after 72 h, suggesting that the films can induce cell proliferation compared to A3 film (a film without CDs). Other studies have reported non-toxicity or low toxicity in CDs (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, & Han, 2022; Lin et al., 2022; Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018; Roy, Ezati, & Rhim, 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trout fish coating with packaging film

ce:section.ce:para: Fish spoilage in uncoated and coated trout fish (Fig. 5 a) is determined by the TVB-N content, a product of bacterial and endogenous enzyme activity. Trimethylamine, dimethylamine, and ammonia are the main components of TVB-N and are produced when proteins and nitrogenous substances are destroyed (Koshy, Koshy, Mary, Sadanandan, Jisha, & Pothan, 2021). TVB-N results are shown in Fig. 5b. The acceptable amount of TVB-N for trout consumption, according to the European Union (EC No. 2074/2005), is 35 mg/100 g (Commission, 2019). The initial value of TVB-N was 7.42 ± 1.23 mg/100 g which increased gradually in all samples (p < 0.05). The amount of TVB-N for uncoated samples exceeded the permissible limit after 4 days, while for the samples coated with A3 and AC-30 films, this phenomenon was observed after 6 and 8 days, respectively. The samples covered with AC-30 film had significantly less TVB-N than others. Given that CDs delayed the growth of the bacteria, it is possible that CDs can prevent the formation of TVB-N. Also, in previous studies, a significant decrease in TVB-N production was observed in pork covered with CD-containing chitosan film during nine days of storage compared to uncoated samples. This decrease was attributed to the antimicrobial property of CDs (Lin et al., 2022).

The secondary oxidation of lipids in unsaturated fatty acids results in an unpleasant taste, an unattractive color, and nutritional loss. Aldehydes, ketones, alcohols, epoxy compounds, organic acids, and other byproducts of secondary oxidation include volatile and nonvolatile molecules. The shelf life of various foods is directly impacted by secondary oxidation. To evaluate this phenomenon, TBARS can be assayed (Candido, Natarelli, Carvalho, & Oliveira, 2022). As shown in Fig. 5c, the TBARS level gradually increased in all samples over time. In the samples coated with films A3 and AC-30, less TBARS was formed compared with the uncoated sample. For the A3 film, this can be explained by the limit imposed on oxygen diffusion to the sample surface; for the AC-30 film, the antioxidant and UV-blocking properties of CDs also could have limited oxidation (Fu et al., 2022).

However, when TBARS exceeded 0.5 mg in all samples, a slight decrease and then an increase was observed, which can be attributed to the destruction of the third degree of malondialdehyde due to the interaction with fish content such as glucose, protein, and amino acids (Shokri & Ehsani, 2017). It should be noted that the TBARS for all samples was less than 1.5 mg/kg during the storage period. In contrast, TBARS exceeding the 2 mg/kg limit typically has a negative effect on the sensory qualities of the stored fish product, and TBARS exceeding 5 mg/kg is considered unacceptable for fresh fish (Kulawik, Jamróz, Zając, Guzik, & Tkaczewska, 2019). The reason for this could be due to the very low amount of fat in trout fish (3.46 % per 100 g), consistent with previous studies (Khoshnoudi‐Nia & Moosavi‐Nasab, 2019).

In this study, oxidized PG was used to improve the physicomechanical properties of gelatin-based films through cross-linkage, and an active packaging film was produced using CD nanoparticles. Oxidized PG showed better TS, WVP, solubility, and heat resistance relative to the gelatin and unoxidized PG/gelatin films. Although adding CDs to the film did not critically affect the TS, WVP, and heat resistance, the AC-30 film offered favorable techno-functional properties such as antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV-blocking activity. Furthermore, the fabricated nanocomposite did not induce cell cytotoxicity. According to the obtained results, by modifying PG and combining it with gelatin and CDs as a functional filler, it is possible to produce biodegradable and inexpensive films for packaging foods that are sensitive to oxidation and vulnerable to bacterial spoilage.
